scenario_name: Scenario 18 — Expert Rule Completeness Under Vague Input
justification: All pass criteria met: 10 business rules extracted with individual confidence reasoning, 6 escalations serving as clarifying questions (demand definition, guardrail values, regulatory compliance, competitive vs profitable conflict, enterprise customers, fallback behavior), 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined and flagged as the single most critical missing piece, acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written), confidence.level is 30 (<=40), and no specific values were invented.

observations.text: 10 well-reasoned business rules extracted, exceeding the minimum of 5, each with invariant classification and justification
Demand identified as undefined in the very first escalation and called 'the single most critical missing piece'
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level of 30 is well within the <=40 threshold
6 escalations cover all expected clarifying questions and more (demand signal, guardrail values, regulatory compliance, competitive vs profitable priority, enterprise customers, fallback behavior)
Added valuable implied rules beyond the expected set: quote-price integrity (BR-8), demand metric consistency (BR-9), and auditability (BR-10)
No specific values invented — all thresholds left explicitly undefined with calls to specify them
Edge cases are thorough and realistic, including demand flapping, regulatory price-gouging, and race conditions
Business rules lack explicit per-rule confidence ratings (high/medium/low) — confidence is discussed in the confidence section but not attached to individual rules

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 implied rules extracted with individual confidence context (exceeds 5 minimum), 6 escalations serving as clarifying questions (exceeds 5 minimum), 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined in multiple places, acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written), overall confidence.level is 30 (<=40), and no specific values were invented without flagging.

observations.text: Extracted 8 well-reasoned business rules, each with invariant classification and justification — exceeds the 5 minimum
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level 30 is appropriately low and well-justified
'Demand' identified as undefined in escalations, ubiquitous_language, and low_confidence_areas — thorough coverage of this critical gap
6 detailed escalations with options and recommendations serve as excellent clarifying questions covering demand definition, guardrails, failover, regulatory, price consistency, and contract pricing
BR-7 (price must never be zero or negative) is a strong addition — correctly classified as a true invariant based on domain knowledge
Edge cases include 7 scenarios, most marked UNSPECIFIED — honest about gaps rather than inventing behavior
Caught the 'probably' qualifier on guardrails as an ambiguity signal — subtle and important
Business impact analysis is thorough with 6 stakeholder groups identified
Assumptions are explicitly listed rather than silently baked into rules

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 rules extracted with individual confidence ratings in high/low confidence areas, 5+ escalations as clarifying questions (demand measurement, guardrail thresholds, regulatory constraints, competitive definition, fallback behavior), 'demand' explicitly identified as undefined (escalation #2), acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then written), overall confidence.level is 30 (<=40), and no specific values were invented without flagging as assumptions.

observations.text: Extracted 8 well-reasoned business rules — exceeds the minimum of 5, with strong invariant justifications
5 detailed escalations with options and recommendations serve as clarifying questions that must be answered before implementation
Demand explicitly called out as undefined in escalation #2 with three concrete options for how to define it
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write Given/When/Then from vague input
Confidence level of 30 with thorough reasoning explaining why nearly every parameter is undefined
No specific values invented — assumptions are clearly labeled as assumptions, not presented as requirements
Edge cases section identifies sophisticated scenarios like algorithmic feedback loops and regulatory price gouging
BR-8 (price-locking on acceptance) is a strong domain inference that shows expert-level thinking beyond what was stated
Excellent distinction between invariants (BR-4/5/6/7/8) and policy rules (BR-1/2/3) with clear justifications

grader_results.detail: field escalations[*].type equals check failed: expected 'ambiguity', got values: 'missing_context', 'missing_context'

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 implied rules extracted (exceeds 5 minimum); 6 detailed escalations surface clarifying questions covering demand definition, guardrail values, quote validity, regulatory compliance, fallback behavior, and competitive-vs-profitable tension (exceeds 5 minimum); 'demand' explicitly called out as undefined in ubiquitous_language, escalations, and low_confidence_areas; acceptance_criteria array is empty (no Given/When/Then criteria written); confidence.level is 30 (<=40); no specific values invented as requirements. The testable_assertion fields within business_rules use Given/When/Then-like phrasing but deliberately include undefined placeholders (e.g., '[undefined SLA]'), reinforcing rather than papering over the ambiguity. Minor note: individual per-rule confidence ratings are not present as discrete fields, but the confidence section effectively categorizes which rules are high vs low confidence.

observations.text: Correctly left acceptance_criteria empty, recognizing the input is too vague for formal criteria
Confidence level of 30 accurately reflects the massive underspecification
Identified 'demand' as undefined in multiple sections — ubiquitous_language, escalations, and low_confidence_areas
Surfaced 6 high-quality escalations covering demand measurement, quote validity, regulatory compliance, fallback behavior, guardrail ambiguity, and competitive-vs-profitable tension
Extracted 8 rules including non-obvious ones like monotonic demand-price relationship (BR-7) and positive price invariant (BR-6)
Correctly classified guardrail existence as invariant while noting specific values are policy — shows strong invariant reasoning
Edge cases are excellent — demand feed failure, rapid oscillation, cross-zone pricing, and guardrail misconfiguration are all highly relevant
Testable assertions deliberately use undefined placeholders rather than inventing values, which is exactly the right behavior
Business rules lack individual per-rule confidence ratings as discrete fields — confidence is addressed globally but not per-rule
